includedSources.name: The Dark Side of the Boom

skippedSources.name: McKinsey & Company (x3: serial builder advantage, frontiers of compute, geopolitical risk readiness)
skippedSources.reason: Empty teaser emails — one-line headline + ‘Also Consider’ links, zero sentences of actual body content

skippedSources.name: cryptonarratives (Best Trades of June 2026)
skippedSources.reason: Product/database description only — describes the structure of a trade database with no actual trade examples or analysis in the email body

items.title: Foreign governments now own the smallest share of US Treasuries in history
items.idea: Foreign government ownership of US Treasuries just hit an all-time low share, even as US debt issuance keeps exploding — the world isn't absorbing the supply at the pace Washington is creating it.
items.angle: This is the “who buys the debt” question in one data point — domestic buyers (Fed, banks, retail) are having to absorb more issuance, which is bullish for gold/hard assets and bearish for the “foreigners will always buy our debt” assumption.
items.tweetA: Foreign governments' share of US Treasury ownership just hit an all-time low. Meanwhile Treasury issuance keeps setting records. Someone has to close that gap — it's either yields rise, the Fed buys, or gold does the job foreigners used to do.
items.tweetB: Every de-dollarization headline gets debated abstractly. This chart just ends the debate: foreign govs own less of the US debt stack than at any point in history. The buyer of last resort isn't abroad anymore — it's the domestic system, and that's a structurally different bond market.

charts.desc: Technical-setups chart on foreign UST ownership share (paywalled) — worth opening

items.title: Market Sentiment's SK Hynix call is up 185% in five months
items.idea: MS valued SK Hynix at €854/share in February when it traded in the low €500s; it's now €1,235 — a 185% return. Memory is 37% of their portfolio and that sleeve alone is +134%. Their Advanced Packaging basket has returned 7.5x QQQ since coverage began.
items.angle: This is a real-money confirmation of the memory/HBM bottleneck thesis that's been playing out all year — worth checking whether the re-rating still has room now that price has blown through the original target.
items.tweetA: MS priced SK Hynix at €854 in Feb when it traded at €518. It's now €1,235 — blowing through their own price target by 45%. When your bull case undershoots reality this badly, the real question is whether anyone's memory-capacity numbers are still valid.
items.tweetB: 37% of a portfolio in memory, +134% return. That's not a trade, that's a conviction bet on the HBM bottleneck thesis — and it paid off harder than even the bulls modeled. The lesson isn't the stock pick, it's sizing when you actually believe the supply/demand math.

items.title: A textbook hedge that didn't work: oil & Texas Pacific Land both fell during the Iran crisis
items.idea: MS put 6% of the portfolio into Texas Pacific Land (down 25%) and USO (down 15%) as an Iran/oil hedge — and both legs lost money anyway, because policy moved to keep markets up and cap oil regardless of the conflict.
items.angle: A real-time lesson that “geopolitical hedges” can fail when there's a political actor actively suppressing the outcome you're hedging for — worth remembering next time a war-premium trade looks obvious.
items.tweetA: MS hedged the Iran war with TPL and USO. Both lost money — down 25% and 15% — because policy put a lid on oil regardless of what Iran did. The lesson: geopolitical hedges fail when there's a political actor actively fighting the outcome you're betting on.
items.tweetB: 6% allocated to ‘war hedge,’ both legs red. Not because the thesis was wrong, but because they hedged the war and not the policy response to the war. Different trade entirely.

items.title: Microsoft and Palantir are both running ‘Us vs Them’ campaigns against Anthropic and OpenAI — with opposite playbooks
items.idea: Nadella (Microsoft, OpenAI's largest investor) wrote “we can't let AI giants eat the economy” while owning a stake worth hundreds of billions in the very company he's warning about. Karp went on CNBC calling the token pricing model “completely wrong” and said enterprises want to “own the means of production” — Palantir jumped 8% on the interview.
items.angle: Enterprise software incumbents are building a coalition narrative against frontier labs right as Anthropic hits $50B+ ARR and OpenAI/Anthropic prep trillion-dollar IPOs — the ‘villain’ framing is a distribution strategy, not a data-privacy stance.
items.tweetA: Palantir jumped 8% because Karp said enterprises want to ‘own the means of production.’ That's Marx's vocabulary, used to sell enterprise software against Anthropic and OpenAI. The performance is the product — and it's working.
items.tweetB: Nadella is warning that ‘AI giants will eat the economy’ while Microsoft holds a stake in OpenAI worth a couple hundred billion dollars. He's not hedging against OpenAI, he's hedging his own optionality — positioning as the trusted broker while owning the thing he's warning about.

items.title: Nvidia just became the enterprise distribution channel for open-source AI to the US government
items.idea: Nvidia's open-source Nemotron models are now shipping into US government agencies through Palantir's customer base — Karp confirmed some government customers have already switched to open-source models. Nvidia is simultaneously the chip supplier and software distributor to Microsoft, Amazon, Google, Oracle, Meta and Palantir all at once.
items.angle: While Microsoft and Palantir fight over ‘who protects enterprise data,’ Nvidia is quietly becoming the arms dealer to every faction — and now the default open-source AI vendor to the federal government, a lane the frontier labs can't easily contest while gated post-‘Blip 2.0.’
items.tweetA: Nvidia isn't picking a side in the Microsoft-vs-Palantir ‘who protects your data’ fight — it's arming both. Now it's shipping open-source Nemotron models straight into US government agencies through Palantir. Jensen doesn't need a side when he's the supplier to every side.
items.tweetB: US government customers are already switching to open-source AI models, per Karp. That's the tell everyone's missing in the frontier-lab-IPO story: the biggest, stickiest enterprise customer on earth may not need OpenAI or Anthropic's frontier models at all.

items.title: Tesla caps employee AI spend at $200/week; Uber burned its entire 2026 AI budget early
items.idea: Per SemiAnalysis's enterprise survey and reporting from The Information/FT, Tesla just capped employee AI token spend at roughly $10K/employee/year right after pushing adoption internally, while Uber reportedly blew through its whole 2026 AI budget ahead of schedule. FT's framing: “We created a monster.”
items.angle: This is the first hard evidence of enterprise AI-spend backlash arriving the same week frontier labs shift from subscription pricing to à la carte — token cost is becoming a real budget line item companies are fighting to control, not a footnote.
items.tweetA: Tesla pushed AI adoption internally, then had to cap employee spend at $200/week. Uber burned its whole 2026 AI budget early. Same corporate pattern every time: give people a powerful tool with no meter, then panic when the bill arrives.
items.tweetB: The AI monetization debate has been about what OpenAI and Anthropic charge. The more interesting number is what enterprises let their own employees spend — and the answer right now is ‘less than they wanted to find out the hard way.’

items.title: Nvidia is turning into a central bank for the AI industry, financing customers with its own balance sheet
items.idea: Nvidia formalized its ‘AI Compute Partnership’ (ACP) — providing GPUs and infrastructure to partners in exchange for a slice of their revenue. The Information's framing: Nvidia acting like a central bank to the very companies buying its chips.
items.angle: This compounds the circularity already dogging the AI capex story (Nvidia investing in OpenAI, OpenAI buying Nvidia chips) — now formalized as revenue-share financing across a wider partner set, meaning Nvidia's balance-sheet risk is increasingly tied to whether its customers' own AI bets pay off.
items.tweetA: Nvidia isn't just selling chips anymore — it's financing the buildout with its own balance sheet in exchange for revenue share. That's not a vendor, that's a central bank to the entire AI compute industry, with cyclical exposure to everyone else's AI bet paying off.
items.tweetB: Every ‘circular AI deal’ story so far has been about OpenAI-Nvidia-Microsoft. The ACP program means Nvidia is now doing this with an expanding list of partners. When the vendor becomes the lender becomes the equity holder, the P&L quality of the whole AI capex complex gets harder to underwrite.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Goldman: Japan's FX intervention is just moving the pressure into the JGB curve
items.idea: Goldman argues FX intervention to prop up the yen can't substitute for actual rate hikes forever — if the Takaichi government doesn't change fiscal stance, Goldman sees further cheapening in 10Y and 30Y JGBs, with pressure spreading into the belly of the curve over time.
items.angle: This is the more precise version of the ‘USD/JPY intervention window’ story everyone's watching — it's really a JGB supply/fiscal story wearing an FX costume, and the belly of the curve is where the next surprise likely shows up.
items.tweetA: Everyone's watching USD/JPY for the next intervention. Goldman's actual point: intervention doesn't fix anything, it just relocates the pressure into 10s and 30s JGBs. The FX market is the release valve on a fiscal problem, not the problem itself.
items.tweetB: Japan is trying to hold back a river at the FX gate. Goldman says the water just finds the JGB curve instead. If you're playing this theme, the interesting trade isn't the yen — it's what happens to the belly of the JGB curve if Takaichi doesn't blink first.

items.title: A CLO ETF yielding 7.5% with a max drawdown of just 6.1%
items.idea: Panagram's CLOZ owns BBB/BB mezzanine CLO tranches (not the risky equity slice), currently distributing ~7.5% with a floating SOFR+spread coupon and only a 6.1% max historical drawdown across ~$816M AUM in 203 tranches. Because it's floating rate, a Fed under Kevin Warsh that holds or hikes is a tailwind, not a threat — the mirror image of REITs/preferreds.
items.angle: With CCC-BB spreads blowing out past 800bps this week (canary for risk-on, per Rosenberg), the mezzanine CLO trade is the ‘get paid while credit stress builds underneath you’ position — worth comparing against the wider high-yield stress signal.
items.tweetA: CLOZ yields 7.5% with a 6.1% max drawdown, and it gets MORE income if the Fed hikes, not less — because it's floating rate. Meanwhile CCC-BB spreads just blew out past 800bps for the first time in 15 months. One of these credit trades benefits from stress, the other signals it.
items.tweetB: Everyone chasing CLO yield buys the equity tranche and gets wrecked when the cycle turns. The institutional version sits in BBB/BB mezz, still floating rate, still ~7.5%, way more subordination underneath it. Yield without reaching for the riskiest slice of the stack.

items.title: 2026's retail stock mania is running almost twice as hot as the 2021 meme-stock rally
items.idea: Per data cited in the newsletter, this year's retail equity mania is running at nearly 2x the intensity of the 2021 meme-stock period, even as crypto lags — with the seasonality stat that ‘there has never been a red July after a red June’ adding a tailwind argument for a rotation into crypto.
items.angle: If retail mania in equities is genuinely outpacing 2021 while crypto stays comparatively muted, that's the setup for a rotation trade rather than a ‘risk-off’ call — the capital hasn't left the casino, it's just sitting in a different room.
items.tweetA: This year's retail stock mania is running at almost 2x the intensity of the 2021 meme-stock rally. Crypto, meanwhile, is stuck in outflow streaks. That's not risk-off — that's retail money sitting in the wrong casino room, waiting for a reason to walk down the hall.
items.tweetB: ‘There's never been a red July after a red June’ is the kind of stat that sounds like a tailwind until you remember sample size gets picked to fit the conclusion. Still, combine it with today's BTC/ETH ETF inflows and NASDAQ's 97.5% correlation to global liquidity, and a summer melt-up isn't crazy.

items.title: The NASDAQ is now 97.5% correlated to total global liquidity
items.idea: A cited stat/chart shows the NASDAQ's correlation to global liquidity measures at 97.5% — an extraordinarily tight relationship implying the index is trading almost purely as a liquidity proxy rather than on fundamentals right now.
items.angle: If that correlation holds, the entire AI-capex-driven tech rally is a liquidity trade wearing a fundamentals costume — the real risk isn't earnings season, it's any central-bank liquidity withdrawal.
items.tweetA: NASDAQ's correlation to global liquidity is sitting at 97.5%. At that level you don't need an earnings thesis to trade the index — you need a central bank balance sheet forecast. The ‘AI fundamentals’ story and the ‘liquidity’ story have basically become the same trade.
items.tweetB: 97.5% correlation to global liquidity is a genuinely wild number — it means fundamentals are contributing almost nothing to the marginal move in the NASDAQ right now. Worth remembering next time someone tells you this rally is about earnings.

charts.desc: Chart of NASDAQ vs. global liquidity index, near-perfect correlation

items.title: Speculators have built the second-largest yen short position in history
items.idea: A cited chart shows speculative JPY short positioning is now the second-largest on record — sitting alongside the Goldman JGB-pressure story as another angle on the same theme: markets are aggressively positioned for continued yen weakness.
items.angle: Crowded positioning plus an active intervention threat is a classic squeeze setup — if Japan steps in hard, or the JGB story forces a policy pivot, this is one of the more violent unwind candidates in G10 macro right now.
items.tweetA: Second-largest speculative yen short in history, stacked right on top of Goldman's call that JGB pressure is building in the belly of the curve. When positioning gets this one-sided AND the policy story is this unstable, the unwind risk isn't small — it's just unpriced.
items.tweetB: Everyone's trading USD/JPY like a rate-differential story. At the second-largest short position ever recorded, it's actually a crowding story now. The crowded trade and the fundamental trade have diverged before, and it usually doesn't end quietly.

charts.desc: Chart of speculative JPY net short positioning, near record highs

items.title: JPMorgan flags Michael Saylor's Strategy as a systemic risk to Bitcoin itself
items.idea: $4.7T JPMorgan has warned that Strategy's (formerly MicroStrategy) leveraged BTC accumulation model is creating a new risk vector for the broader Bitcoin market.
items.angle: Worth tracking against the ETF outflow data — a forced-selling event hitting Strategy's balance sheet would compound exactly the kind of one-sided outflow streak already showing up in the ETF flow charts.
items.tweetA: JPMorgan just named Michael Saylor's Strategy as a systemic risk to Bitcoin. Translation: the market's biggest corporate BTC whale, levered up, is now itself a tail risk to the asset it was supposed to be validating.
items.tweetB: The bull case for corporate BTC treasuries was always ‘more demand, less float, higher price.’ JPMorgan's warning flips that framing — concentrated leveraged holders can just as easily become the forced seller that breaks the market on the way down.

items.title: Anthropic is reportedly in talks with Samsung for a custom AI chip
items.idea: Brief report that Anthropic is negotiating with Samsung on custom silicon — following the pattern of OpenAI/Broadcom and Google/TPU, another frontier lab moving to reduce Nvidia dependency.
items.angle: Combined with SK Hynix's rebound this week on the same Anthropic-Samsung headline (per Dark Side of the Boom's Weekender), this is a live, developing memory/foundry story worth tracking into Samsung's July 7 earnings.
items.tweetA: Anthropic is now in custom-chip talks with Samsung. That's three of the four major frontier labs (OpenAI/Broadcom, Google/TPU, now Anthropic/Samsung) building their own silicon. Nvidia's moat isn't the chip anymore, it's the software stack around it — and that's the part getting tested next.
items.tweetB: This headline alone helped drag SK Hynix and the broader Korean chip complex out of a two-day, 30% drawdown this week. Custom silicon talk isn't just a cost story for Anthropic — it's a demand signal the memory market is already trading on.

items.title: Agility Robotics is going public at a $2.5B valuation — the first pure-play robotics IPO
items.idea: Agility Robotics (maker of the Digit humanoid robot) is IPOing at $2.5B, marking what's billed as the first pure robotics IPO of this cycle.
items.angle: This is the opening bell for public-market humanoid/robotics exposure — worth watching as the comp set (and valuation benchmark) for every other robotics startup currently raising private rounds.
items.tweetA: First pure-play robotics IPO of this cycle just priced at $2.5B. Every humanoid robotics startup currently raising a private round just got a public comp — and a valuation ceiling test — for free.
items.tweetB: Robotics has been a venture story for years while AI got all the public-market attention. A $2.5B IPO changes that — now there's a market price for ‘humanoid robots, standalone,’ and every VC round after this gets marked against it.

items.title: A new benchmark shows Fable 5 matching human professionals in just 16.1% of real freelance tasks
items.idea: Per a cited SafeAI blog post, Fable 5 matches human professional performance in only 16.1% of tested freelance tasks — despite frontier-model marketing suggesting near-human competence broadly.
items.angle: A useful reality check against the ‘AI replaces knowledge work next year’ narrative — real task-level benchmarks still show a large gap between frontier model capability and professional-grade output on unstructured freelance work.
items.tweetA: 16.1% is the number nobody's tweeting: that's how often a frontier model actually matches a human professional on real freelance tasks, per a new benchmark. The AGI-in-two-years narrative and the actual task-completion data are not yet the same conversation.
items.tweetB: Every ‘AI takes your job’ headline should come with this benchmark attached. 16.1% task-match rate against human professionals is real progress — and also a long way from the labor-market disruption already being priced into some stocks.

items.title: Russia's central bank says the digital ruble is ready for wider rollout starting September 1
items.idea: Russia's CBDC (digital ruble) has been confirmed ready for broader use starting Sept 1, per the central bank governor.
items.angle: A data point in the broader de-dollarization / CBDC race — worth tracking alongside China's digital yuan rollout as a signal of how fast state-backed digital currency infrastructure is actually maturing versus just being announced.
items.tweetA: Russia's digital ruble goes live for wider use September 1. Every CBDC launch gets treated as a non-event until enough of them stack up that the alternative payment rail is just... there. This is one more brick.
items.tweetB: The dollar's plumbing dominance gets debated in the abstract constantly. The concrete version is a rolling list of go-live dates for state digital currencies outside the dollar system — Sept 1 just got added to that list.

items.title: Endurance Gold is building gold ounces in the ground for C$9/oz
items.idea: Endurance Gold's Reliance Project in BC's Bridge River camp holds an inferred resource of 1.45M oz at 2.30 g/t, built at a discovery cost of just C$9 per ounce — an exceptionally low number for junior gold exploration.
items.angle: With gold's structural bull case intact (central banks still buying, per the Goldman note below), C$9/oz discovery-cost juniors are the leveraged, high-optionality way to play a continued gold re-rating — worth screening the space for similarly cheap names.
items.tweetA: C$9 per ounce discovery cost. That's not a typo — that's what Endurance Gold is spending to add inferred gold ounces at its Reliance project. In a market where producers pay $1,500+/oz to buy ounces via M&A, that's the kind of asymmetry junior gold investors dream about.
items.tweetB: Everyone's playing gold through GLD or the majors. The actual convexity is in juniors adding ounces at C$9 apiece while the metal sits near record highs — that gap between discovery cost and spot price is the entire thesis in one number.

charts.desc: Endurance Gold daily chart (basing at support) — worth opening

items.title: The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve is at its lowest level since 1983 — with oil calm that ‘hasn't earned it’
items.idea: Oil has retreated below $70, but the buffer behind that calm is gone: the SPR sits at its lowest level since 1983, and the author argues the market has almost no cushion left if the Iran peace deal falters — with tanker warnings out of the Strait of Hormuz still live.
items.angle: A calm oil tape sitting on top of a nearly-empty strategic reserve is a fragile setup, not a stable one — the risk/reward on downside oil hedges (long vol, or long upside calls) looks mispriced if Hormuz headline risk resumes.
items.tweetA: Oil under $70 looks calm. The SPR at its lowest level since 1983 says the calm is unearned — there's almost no strategic buffer left if the Iran deal cracks. This is a market pricing stability while sitting on an empty spare tank.
items.tweetB: Nobody's pricing in the SPR number. A 1983-level reserve plus live tanker warnings out of Hormuz is exactly the setup where a single headline reprices oil $20+ overnight — and this time there's no reserve release left to soften it.

items.title: The real China story isn't ‘China Shock 2.0’ — it's a monetary systems war, and China needs to devalue in gold terms
items.idea: The thesis: China doesn't need to revalue the yuan against the dollar, it needs to devalue it in real/gold terms to ease domestic debt deflation while defending its managed currency system — which structurally supports higher gold prices as the mechanism for that devaluation.
items.angle: This reframes the entire ‘de-dollarization’ narrative — it's not really yuan vs dollar, it's both superpowers using gold as the pressure valve for their own domestic monetary problems, which is bullish gold regardless of who ‘wins.’
items.tweetA: The framing everyone's using — ‘China Shock 2.0’ — misses the actual mechanism. China doesn't need a stronger yuan against the dollar, it needs a weaker yuan against GOLD to ease its debt deflation. That's a devaluation that shows up as a rising gold price, not a falling yuan.
items.tweetB: Two monetary systems, same escape valve: both the US (dollar-Treasury order funding AI capex) and China (managed yuan easing debt deflation) end up needing gold to do work for them. That's not a coincidence — that's why gold keeps grinding higher regardless of who's ‘winning’ the capital war.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Goldman: the AI buildout could require $7 trillion in financing between 2026 and 2031
items.idea: Goldman Sachs Investment Banking estimates the AI infrastructure buildout could require roughly $7T over the next five years — pushing financing well beyond hyperscaler capex, into equity markets, public/private debt, sovereign wealth, pension capital and JV structures.
items.angle: $7T is larger than the entire private credit market today — this explains why every capital pool (SWFs, pensions, infra funds) is suddenly an ‘AI investor,’ and why the AI story is shifting from a tech-sector story to a capital-markets story.
items.tweetA: $7 trillion. That's Goldman's estimate for what the AI buildout needs through 2031 — more than the entire private credit market currently holds. This is why sovereign wealth funds and pensions are suddenly ‘AI infrastructure investors’: there's literally no other pool of capital big enough.
items.tweetB: The AI story stopped being about chips and models a while ago. At a $7T financing need, it's now a capital-markets allocation story — and the winners won't just be the best technology, they'll be whoever can actually secure that scale of capital before the cycle turns.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Hyperscalers have issued $107B in debt this year — already more than all of 2025, and 5x 2024
items.idea: Hyperscale companies had issued ~$107B of debt by June 23, already above 2025's full-year total and far larger than the <$20B raised in 2024 — the AI buildout is shifting from an equity-funded story to a credit-market story.
items.angle: This is the concrete data point behind every ‘is AI capex sustainable’ debate — the financing mix is visibly shifting toward leverage, which changes the risk profile from ‘growth stock drawdown’ to ‘credit-cycle risk’ if utilization or returns disappoint.
items.tweetA: $107B in hyperscaler debt issued by June 23 — more than ALL of 2025, and over 5x what was raised in 2024. The AI trade quietly stopped being an equity story and became a credit story. Nobody's repricing the risk premium for that shift yet.
items.tweetB: Free cash flow funded the first phase of the AI buildout. Debt is funding this phase. That's the single most important shift in the entire AI capex story this year, and it's happening almost silently next to the headline GPU numbers.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: A record 45% of central banks plan to increase gold holdings in the next 12 months
items.idea: A World Gold Council survey of 76 central banks (Feb-May) found 45% expect to increase gold holdings over the next year — the highest reading in the survey's history. Goldman's year-end gold target is $4,900, even after a ~$300 pullback to ~$4,016 in H1.
items.angle: Central-bank buying is the structural floor under gold regardless of the Fed path — this survey reading hitting an all-time high while gold just had its worst first half in years is the classic ‘weak hands sell into strong hands accumulating’ setup.
items.tweetA: 45% of central banks plan to buy more gold in the next 12 months — the highest reading ever recorded in this survey. Gold just had its worst first half in years. Those two facts together mean retail and macro funds sold the exact dip central banks are about to buy.
items.tweetB: Gold gave back $300 in H1 as the market priced a hawkish Fed. Nothing in the structural story changed: central banks are diversifying at a record pace, per the WGC survey. Goldman's $4,900 year-end target isn't a rate call, it's a reserve-manager call.

charts.desc: World Gold Council central-bank-buying-intent survey chart, all-time high reading

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: $17.2B left US equity funds in a week while Japan just had its best inflow in seven weeks
items.idea: Per BofA flow data, US investors pulled $17.2B from domestic equity funds in the week through July 1, while Japanese equities drew their largest inflow in seven weeks — even as the Stoxx 600 grinds toward record highs on utilities/tech.
items.angle: This is a rotation signal hiding inside a ‘still bullish AI’ narrative — capital isn't fleeing risk, it's fleeing US concentration risk and paying up for cheaper international beta on the same softer-dollar, easier-Fed thesis.
items.tweetA: $17.2B out of US equity funds in one week. Japan's best inflow in 7 weeks in the same stretch. This isn't a risk-off signal — it's a ‘the US AI trade got too expensive, Japan/Europe are the cheaper way to buy the same macro thesis’ signal.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching the AI-momentum wobble in US mega-cap tech. The quieter story is where the money actually went: not to cash, to Japan and Europe. The global liquidity trade is still on, just not concentrated in the same five US names anymore.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Goldman: the S&P's entire 21% return this year has been earnings-driven — there's no valuation cushion left
items.idea: Per Goldman strategist Ben Snider, the market's 21% one-year return has been entirely earnings-driven. Consensus expects Q2 S&P EPS up 22% y/y; Goldman forecasts 24% full-year 2026 EPS growth — leaving essentially zero room for disappointment given valuations are already full.
items.angle: This sets up Q2 earnings (week of July 13) as a binary catalyst for the whole market, not just AI names — worth positioning volatility around the print dates rather than assuming a benign summer drift.
items.tweetA: The entire 21% S&P return this year came from earnings, not multiple expansion, per Goldman. That sounds healthy until you realize it means there's zero valuation cushion left if Q2 EPS (consensus +22%) disappoints even slightly.
items.tweetB: Goldman's own 2026 EPS forecast is 24% growth. Consensus is 22%. When the sell-side's own number is ABOVE consensus, that's not caution, that's a house calling for a beat-and-raise summer. Fade that at your own risk into July 13.

items.title: A nine-year Open Philanthropy (now Coefficient Giving) veteran on leaving — and never having built anything that lasted two years
items.idea: The former head of an ~45-person AI team at Open Philanthropy/Coefficient Giving describes a decade-long cycle of grand visions collapsing within 6-18 months, moving through EA community building, AI risk assessment at METR, and alignment research — while admitting she still doesn't know what the highest-impact thing to work on is, even after leaving.
items.angle: A rare, unvarnished look inside the psychology of the people setting the AI safety funding and research agenda at institutions (Open Phil, METR) that influence AI policy discourse — worth reading as a sentiment indicator on how confident the ‘AI safety establishment’ actually is in its own roadmap.
items.tweetA: A nine-year Open Philanthropy/Coefficient Giving veteran who ran a 45-person AI team just admitted, on leaving, that she never built anything that lasted two years and still doesn't know what the right AI safety bet is. If the people funding AI safety research are this uncertain internally, price that into every ‘the safety community has a plan’ narrative.
items.tweetB: This essay is the inside view of the institutions writing AI risk assessments that move policy — and it reads less like confidence, more like a decade of expensive trial and error. Worth remembering next time a think-tank report gets treated as settled science.

items.title: The US led in 60 of 64 critical technologies a few years ago. Today it leads in 7 — China leads in 57
items.idea: Per a16z's American Dynamism essay, the US used to lead in 60 of the 64 most critical technologies; today the US leads in only 7, with China leading in 57. Meanwhile, defense-tech startups are reaching their first $100M government program in under 5 years (Anduril: 3 years, Saronic: under 3 years) versus ~30 years for legacy primes.
items.angle: That 60-to-7 flip is a starker number than most China-tech-race headlines convey — but it's paired with genuinely fast-cycling defense-tech startups (Anduril, Saronic) compressing procurement timelines by 10x, which is the actual policy/investment bet a16z is making to close the gap.
items.tweetA: The US led in 60 of 64 critical technologies a few years ago. Today: 7. China leads in 57. That flip happened faster than almost any technology-leadership reversal in modern history — and it's the actual number behind every ‘American Dynamism’ venture thesis right now.
items.tweetB: Anduril hit its first $100M government program in 3 years. Legacy defense primes used to take 30 years for the same milestone. That's the bet embedded in every defense-tech venture round today: the 60-to-7 technology gap gets closed by compressing procurement cycles 10x, not by out-funding China dollar for dollar.

charts.desc: a16z chart on US vs China lead in 64 critical technologies, 2019 vs today

items.title: A defense-tech shipyard startup is paying 46% above the local wage average in rural Louisiana
items.idea: Saronic's Louisiana shipyard expansion is projected to add ~3,270 jobs at an average annual pay of ~$88K — about 46% above the parish average, in a region where manufacturing jobs used to not require a college degree to support a family.
items.angle: A concrete, geographically specific data point on reshoring wage effects — worth tracking as a leading indicator for whether the ‘bring manufacturing home’ venture thesis actually produces measurable local wage inflation before it shows up in national data.
items.tweetA: 3,270 jobs, $88K average pay, 46% above the local parish average — that's what one defense-tech shipyard startup is doing to rural Louisiana wages. This is the reshoring thesis showing up as an actual regional labor-market data point, not a talking point.
items.tweetB: Forget the national reshoring debate for a second. A 46% local wage premium in a single parish is the kind of number that shows up in a Fed regional survey before it shows up in the CPI print. Worth watching Gulf Coast wage data over the next few quarters.

items.title: Bitcoin ETFs just recorded their longest streak of consecutive outflow days since launch
items.idea: Since May, BTC ETF inflow streaks have never exceeded a single day, while outflow streaks have repeatedly run for days — culminating in the longest outflow run since the ETFs launched. Today's single inflow day, and the price bounce to $60K+, doesn't change that pattern.
items.angle: This directly contradicts the ‘institutional demand is back’ framing every time BTC bounces — the flow data says the opposite: one good day sitting on top of the worst structural outflow trend on record.
items.tweetA: Longest streak of BTC ETF outflows since the ETFs launched — and it's happening WHILE bitcoin bounces back above $60K. Price and flows are telling two different stories right now, and flows are usually the one that's right.
items.tweetB: One inflow day after weeks of persistent outflows is not ‘demand is back,’ it's noise sitting on top of a trend. The bar for calling this a real recovery isn't a green day, it's several consecutive weeks of inflows — and we're nowhere close.

charts.desc: Chart of consecutive BTC ETF inflow/outflow streaks since May, longest outflow run on record

items.title: Ethereum ETF flows confirm it: capital isn't rotating out of Bitcoin, it's leaving crypto entirely
items.idea: Comparing cumulative BTC and ETH ETF net flows shows both telling the same story — steady capital outflow for months. Bitcoin isn't losing share to Ethereum; the whole asset class is seeing reduced institutional appetite.
items.angle: This kills the ‘rotation into alts’ narrative that tends to circulate whenever BTC dips — the ETF flow data says there's no rotation happening, just broad-based de-risking across the entire crypto ETF complex.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin ETF outflows aren't rotating into Ethereum ETFs — both are bleeding at the same time. The ‘money is just rotating into alts’ story doesn't survive contact with the actual flow data. This is broad de-risking, not a reallocation.
items.tweetB: Strip out the volatility difference between BTC and ETH ETFs and the flow picture is identical: months of steady capital leaving. When two assets with different investor bases both bleed the same way, that's a macro allocation decision, not an asset-specific story.

items.title: TIPS now pay 2%+ real yield — the first time in 15 years safe assets have offered real income
items.idea: Investors can now earn 2%+ above inflation holding inflation-protected US government bonds — a real yield environment that simply didn't exist for most of the past 15 years, when real yields averaged zero and investors were pushed further out the risk curve (including into BTC) to get returns.
items.angle: This is an underrated headwind for the ‘digital gold’ bitcoin thesis right now — a genuinely competitive, truly risk-free real-yield alternative didn't exist during BTC's entire bull-market history until recently, and it's not going away just because rates eventually get cut.
items.tweetA: 2%+ real yields on TIPS is a genuinely new phenomenon — that didn't exist for basically all of Bitcoin's bull market history. The ‘there is no yield alternative’ argument for BTC as digital gold just got a real, risk-free competitor for the first time in 15 years.
items.tweetB: Bitcoin's entire post-2010 rise happened in a world of ~0% real yields, where capital had nowhere else to go for income. That world just ended. Whatever BTC's next leg looks like, it has to win against an actual risk-free 2%+ real return now — a fight it's never had to have before.

items.title: GFL Environmental just did the oldest trick in the book: dropping news on the Friday before a market holiday
items.idea: Herb Greenberg flags that GFL — a company he's covered for two years amid ‘drama and dysfunction’ — released news on the Friday before July 4th, a market holiday, historically the go-to slot for companies burying bad news (his example: Computer Associates' 1999 auditor-change 8-K, filed on a closed-market Friday amid accounting scrutiny).
items.angle: The specific GFL news is paywalled, but the pattern-recognition itself is the trade signal — when a stock you're watching drops an 8-K or press release on a holiday Friday, that's rarely a coincidence, and it's worth flagging GFL for a Monday read-through.
items.tweetA: Company you're watching drops news the Friday before a market holiday? That's not a coincidence, that's a 27-year-old playbook — Computer Associates did it in 1999 with an auditor-change 8-K during an accounting scandal. GFL just ran the same play today.
items.tweetB: The ‘holiday Friday news dump’ is one of the most reliable tells in small/mid-cap land — nobody's watching, markets are closed, and the story is stale by Monday's open. If GFL is doing it again, the read isn't ‘what did they say,’ it's ‘why did they need nobody watching.’

items.title: The Space Race everyone remembers ended in a 1975 handshake — a forgotten precedent for the US-China AI race
items.idea: The US-USSR Space Race (Sputnik 1957 to the last moonwalk in 1972) is remembered as pure rivalry, but it was followed by the Apollo-Soyuz handshake (1975), then Shuttle-Mir, then 25+ years and $200B+ of joint US-Russia investment running the ISS together — through every geopolitical low point since.
items.angle: Every US-China AI narrative defaults to the competitive ‘Us vs Them’ framing — this is the historical counter-case: the two Cold War superpowers built more together in space than they ever did apart, worth asking whether AI infrastructure eventually follows the same arc once the competitive phase burns out.
items.tweetA: Everyone remembers the US-USSR Space Race as pure rivalry. What gets forgotten: it ended in a 1975 handshake, then 25+ years and $200B of joint investment running the ISS together — through every Cold War low point since. Worth remembering before assuming the US-China AI race only has one ending.
items.tweetB: The ‘Us vs Them’ AI narrative (Nadella vs frontier labs, US vs China) is the oldest coalition tool in history — city-states, religions, Cold War blocs, now software markets. But the actual historical base rate says these rivalries end in more collaboration than the headlines ever suggest, not less.

items.title: Stripe's stablecoin platform Bridge secures MiCA and EMI licenses in Luxembourg
items.idea: Stripe's Bridge has secured MiCA (Markets in Crypto-Assets) and Electronic Money Institution licenses in Luxembourg — giving it a regulated EU stablecoin/payments footprint.
items.angle: This is one of the clearest ‘TradFi payments giant goes fully licensed in stablecoins’ moves yet — worth tracking against Circle/USDC's institutional partnerships as the regulated-stablecoin land grab in Europe accelerates.
items.tweetA: Stripe just got its stablecoin arm MiCA + EMI licensed in Luxembourg. That's not a crypto company chasing regulatory arbitrage — that's a $70B+ payments giant building fully licensed stablecoin rails inside the EU. Pay attention to who else follows.
items.tweetB: Every stablecoin regulation story gets framed as restriction. Stripe getting licensed in Luxembourg is the opposite signal: the biggest payments companies now see enough certainty in the EU framework to build real infrastructure on top of it.

items.title: OKX saw $1.199B of inflows as major exchanges pull back from Europe
items.idea: OKX recorded $1.199B of inflows as major crypto exchanges began removing services from parts of Europe, per cited exchange flow data.
items.angle: A regulatory-driven market-share reshuffle among exchanges is worth tracking — capital is concentrating toward whichever venues stay compliant and operational as European crypto rules tighten.
items.tweetA: $1.2B flowed into OKX the same week major exchanges started pulling services out of Europe. When regulation forces an exit, the flow doesn't disappear — it just finds whichever exchange is still standing. Worth watching who else picks up share.
items.tweetB: Regulatory retreat from Europe isn't shrinking crypto volumes, it's just redistributing them. $1.2B into one exchange in a single window is a bigger signal about market structure than about OKX specifically.

items.title: Forward Industries added 500,000 SOL to its treasury last week
items.idea: Forward Industries acquired 500,000 SOL last week, adding to a corporate treasury strategy modeled on the MicroStrategy/Strategy Bitcoin playbook, but applied to Solana.
items.angle: Worth tracking alongside the JPMorgan warning on Strategy above — the corporate-treasury-as-leveraged-crypto-vehicle playbook is now spreading beyond Bitcoin into altcoins, which changes the risk profile of that whole trade.
items.tweetA: Forward Industries just bought 500,000 SOL for its corporate treasury — the MicroStrategy playbook, now running on Solana. The same week JPMorgan flagged Strategy's BTC leverage as a systemic risk. Watch how fast this copy-trade spreads to more tickers before someone gets it wrong.
items.tweetB: Corporate treasury crypto plays used to mean one company, one asset, one thesis (Saylor, Bitcoin). Now it's spreading to SOL. When the playbook gets copied across assets this fast, the risk isn't the asset — it's the leverage stacking up behind all of them at once.

items.title: Sam Altman says AI will reach superintelligence in two years — and ‘everyone loses jobs’
items.idea: Cited remarks attributed to Sam Altman project superintelligence within two years, with an accompanying warning that the transition costs widespread job losses.
items.angle: Whether or not the timeline holds, a frontier-lab CEO publicly pairing ‘superintelligence in 2 years’ with ‘everyone loses jobs’ is itself a signal worth tracking — it's a very different public posture than the usual soft-pedaled AI-safety messaging from the same labs.
items.tweetA: The CEO of the company racing hardest toward AGI just said, on the record, that superintelligence arrives in two years and ‘everyone loses jobs.’ That's not a doomer blogger, that's the person setting the pace of the race saying the quiet part out loud.
items.tweetB: Compare this to the 16.1% human-parity benchmark story from the same week: one number says frontier AI barely matches professionals on a sixth of real tasks, the other says the same industry's leading voice expects superintelligence in 24 months. Someone's timeline is wrong — the question is which one.
